gm.name: Brian Gutekunst
gm.title: General Manager
gm.previous_role: Director of Player Personnel, Green Bay Packers

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Ted Thompson (direct boss for nearly 20 years) / Ron Wolf (organizational founder of the Packers' scouting philosophy)
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Ron Wolf tree → Ted Thompson branch. Wolf built the Packers' modern scouting infrastructure and instilled a 'draft and develop' philosophy with an emphasis on big, fast, athletic prospects. Thompson refined this with extreme patience in free agency and an almost religious commitment to BPA drafting. Gutekunst inherited both influences but has been more willing to engage in free agency and trades than Thompson ever was. The Wolf tree also produced John Schneider (SEA), John Dorsey (KC/CLE), Reggie McKenzie (OAK), and Scot McCloughan (WAS/SF), all of whom share an emphasis on elite athleticism and size at the point of evaluation.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: More active in free agency than Thompson — signed Za'Darius Smith, Adrian Amos (2019), Josh Jacobs, Xavier McKinney (2024), Aaron Banks, Nate Hobbs (2025). More willing to trade up in the first round (traded up in 4 of first 6 drafts). Thompson almost never traded up. Gutekunst also traded Davante Adams and Aaron Rodgers — bold roster-shaping trades Thompson likely would not have made. However, the core evaluation DNA — prioritizing elite athleticism, size, youth, and versatility — remains identical to the Wolf/Thompson lineage.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: 8 drafts of his own data make the lineage weight very low as a predictive signal. Gutekunst's own track record is now the dominant signal. The Wolf/Thompson DNA is baked into everything he does but his own patterns are well-established.

key_influencers.name: Matt LaFleur
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded, runs a Shanahan/McVay-style outside zone scheme. Values pass protection for Jordan Love. Significant input on defensive coordinator hires (chose Hafley, then Gannon) which shapes defensive personnel needs. Has described the Gannon scheme as 'genius.' Collaborative relationship with Gutekunst.

key_influencers.name: Jon-Eric Sullivan (departed Jan 2026)
key_influencers.title: Former Vice President of Player Personnel (now Miami GM)
key_influencers.known_biases: Was a hot name on interview circuit. Hired as Dolphins GM in Jan 2026 after 20+ years with Packers. His departure is notable — he was a key part of the evaluation apparatus. New VP of Player Personnel TBD.

key_influencers.name: John Wojciechowski
key_influencers.title: Director of Player Personnel
key_influencers.known_biases: Identified as a future GM candidate. Rising internal evaluator within the Wolf tree system.

key_influencers.name: Jonathan Gannon
key_influencers.title: Defensive Coordinator (hired Feb 2026)
key_influencers.known_biases: New DC replacing Jeff Hafley. Will run a 3-4 base with quarters coverage emphasis and three-safety looks. Prefers scheme versatility and coverage disguises. His scheme demands will influence defensive draft targets — particularly versatile safeties, speed/bend edge rushers, and cornerbacks who can play multiple coverages.

front_office.power_dynamics: Collaborative front office where Gutekunst has final say but LaFleur has significant input, especially on offensive personnel. The Packers are a publicly-owned franchise with no single owner to interfere. Team President Mark Murphy (now departed) and successor have historically stayed out of football decisions. Gutekunst and LaFleur signed simultaneous contract extensions in Jan 2026, signaling organizational alignment.
front_office.owner_interference: none — publicly owned franchise, unique in NFL

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Best player available, 'build through the draft,' draft-and-develop philosophy inherited from Ted Thompson. Gutekunst has said 'traits are very, very important to us' and emphasizes that 'there are certain athletic traits you have to have to compete at the level we want to.'
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Leans BPA in round 1 but with a strong athleticism/traits filter that functionally limits the pool. Seven of nine first-round picks had RAS of 9.0+; eight of nine were 22 or younger. In rounds 2-3, shifts notably toward need-filling — drafted skill position players on offense in six straight second-round picks (2019-2023) when the roster needed them, then pivoted to defense in 2024 when the secondary was the biggest hole. Has a pattern of doubling up at positions of need (2 WRs in 2022, 2 WRs + 2 TEs in 2023, 3 safeties in 2024, 2 WRs in 2025). This doubling-up strategy is a signature Gutekunst move.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Claims BPA but the 2020 draft (Love, Dillon, Deguara) was widely perceived as ignoring immediate needs. The 2022 draft leaned heavily toward filling gaps left by the Davante Adams trade. In 2024, every pick in rounds 1-4 addressed a position of need. The mismatch has narrowed in recent years — Gutekunst now more openly integrates need into his BPA framework.

trade_triggers: QB availability — traded up for Jordan Love in 2020
First-round target sliding — traded up for Jaire Alexander (2018), Darnell Savage (2019), Lukas Van Ness (2023)
Acquiring future capital — traded down from 14 to 27 in 2018 to gain an extra 2019 first-rounder
Blockbuster player acquisition — traded two 1sts and Kenny Clark for Micah Parsons in Aug 2025
Day 2 pick consolidation — frequently trades to move up within rounds 2-3
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Willing to spend future picks for elite talent (Parsons trade). Historically values having quantity of picks but will consolidate aggressively for targets. Traded up in first round in 4 of first 6 drafts. Also leveraged Rodgers and Adams trades to acquire significant draft capital. Entering 2026 without a first-round pick for first time due to Parsons trade.

position_avoidance: Has never drafted a RB in round 1 but took AJ Dillon in round 2 (2020) and MarShawn Lloyd in round 3 (2024) — willing to invest Day 2 capital
Has never drafted a WR in round 1 until Matthew Golden in 2025 — a 23-year drought at the position in round 1
Has never drafted a TE higher than round 3
position_premium: EDGE — drafted Rashan Gary (1.12), Lukas Van Ness (1.13), and also traded for Micah Parsons. Highest positional priority.
CB — drafted Jaire Alexander (1.18), Eric Stokes (1.29), plus multiple Day 3 CBs. Consistent early investment.
Defense in round 1 — 7 of 10 first-round picks have been defensive players
Safety — unprecedented 3 safeties in 2024 draft (Bullard Rd2, Williams Rd4, Oladapo Rd5)
OL — drafted 3 OL in 2020, 3 OL in 2022. Frequently invests heavily in offensive line

EDGE.archetype_summary: Massive, elite-athleticism power rusher with length and upside. Gutekunst drafts EDGEs who are physical specimens — 6-3+ tall, 255+ lbs, 34+ inch arms, sub-4.60 40s at their weight. Both first-round EDGEs (Gary, Van Ness) were raw prospects with underwhelming college sack production relative to their draft position but possessed elite physical tools. Gutekunst bets on traits over production at this position. He also supplemented via trade (Parsons) and free agency (Za'Darius Smith). The archetype is clear: big, explosive, developmental with motor and length.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34 inches — both Gary (34.13) and Van Ness (34.00) meet this threshold
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash (sub-4.60 for both), vertical jump (Gary 38.0), broad jump (Gary 120 in, Van Ness 118 in). Explosion tests matter most. Speed Score (weight-adjusted 40) extremely important given size requirements.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.60 or faster — Gary 4.58, Van Ness 4.58. Both hit exact same time at massive weights.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Power and explosion — both Gary and Van Ness were drafted as power-first rushers who win with violent hands and bull rush, not finesse or bend
most_valued_traits: First-step explosion
Length and long arms for tackle-radius disruption
Power at the point of attack
Motor and relentless effort
Scheme versatility — can stand up or hand in dirt
least_important_traits: Refined pass rush repertoire — has drafted EDGEs who were raw and needed NFL coaching to develop moves
College sack production — Gary had only 3.5 sacks his final season at Michigan
deal_breakers: Lack of elite physical tools — pure technique/finesse rushers without top-end athleticism not valued in round 1
Short arms — 34 inches appears to be a hard minimum
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite RAS / athleticism score. Gary was the #1 overall recruit and RAS 9.95. Van Ness RAS 9.39. Gutekunst will reach for physical tools.

college_production.production_floor: No clear floor — Gary had only 3.5 sacks his final season. Van Ness had 6.5 sacks as a sophomore. Production is secondary to traits at this position.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending production (Van Ness went from 4 sacks to 8 sacks) but will tolerate a dip if explained by injury or scheme (Gary's senior year).
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — credits pressure rates and TFLs, not just sack totals. Gary generated pressures that didn't convert to sacks. Van Ness played in Iowa's conservative defense.

examples: 2019 1.12 — Rashan Gary: 6-5, 277, 4.58 40, 34.13 arms, RAS 9.95. Michigan. 3.5 sacks in final year. Elite athlete, raw pass rusher. Now a Pro Bowler.
2023 1.13 — Lukas Van Ness: 6-5, 272, 4.58 40, 34.00 arms, RAS 9.39. Iowa. 6.5 sacks as sophomore. Power-first, violent hands, limited experience.
2025 4.124 — Barryn Sorrell: 6-3, 256. Texas. 15.5 career sacks over 3 seasons. Developmental Day 3 EDGE.
2025 5.159 — Collin Oliver: Oklahoma State. Early producer (11.5 sacks as freshman). Pass-rush specialist type.

CB.archetype_summary: Tall (5-10+), fast (4.45 or faster), high-RAS press-capable corner. The Packers have a historically documented minimum height threshold at CB dating back to the Ron Wolf era. Jaire Alexander at 5-10 1/4 is the shortest CB drafted since 2005. Weight minimum is approximately 184 lbs. They value speed first and foremost but have drafted across the athletic spectrum otherwise. Ball production in college is NOT required — film and traits matter more. Press-man capability is strongly preferred.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-10 1/4 to 6-2 — 5-10 1/4 is the absolute floor (Alexander). Preference is 5-11+.
physical_profile.weight_range: 184–202 lbs. Jean-Charles (184) is the lightest. Preference is 190+.
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear hard cutoff but 31+ inches preferred for outside corners. Slot corners can be shorter-armed.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash is paramount. Alexander 4.38, Stokes 4.31, Valentine 4.44, Josh Jackson 4.56 (the slowest). RAS scores: Alexander 9.53, Stokes 9.35, Valentine 9.29. Gutekunst explicitly wants high-RAS corners.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 absolute maximum for outside corners. Ideally 4.45 or faster. Stokes' 4.31 was a standout.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Press-man capability required. Valentine described himself as 'a physical press-man corner.' Stokes was drafted for his speed to play man. Alexander is an elite press-man technician.
most_valued_traits: Speed — non-negotiable at the position
Press technique and physicality at the line
Length and disruption at catch point
Versatility to play multiple coverage types
Tackling — size matters in part for this reason
least_important_traits: Interception totals — Stokes had just 1 INT in college, Valentine had modest numbers. Film matters more than box-score ball production.
deal_breakers: Height under 5-10 — Mike Sainristil (5-9) was explicitly identified as someone NOT on GB's board despite being a top prospect
Weight under 184 lbs — has only violated once (Jean-Charles, a bust)
Slow 40 time — never drafted a CB over 4.56 in rounds 1-3
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite 40 time combined with adequate size. Stokes' 4.31 40 at 6-1, 194 was a profile Gutekunst couldn't pass up at 29.

college_production.production_floor: No clear statistical floor — values film over INT totals. Stokes had just 1 INT in his final season. Valentine had 2 INTs across 3 years.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers multi-year starters — Valentine started 25 consecutive games at Kentucky. Stokes started 2 years at Georgia.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — adjusts for conference strength (SEC/Big Ten corners valued) and scheme.

examples: 2018 1.18 — Jaire Alexander: 5-10 1/4, 196, 4.38 40, RAS 9.53. Louisville. Traded up to get him. Elite press corner, 2x All-Pro.
2021 1.29 — Eric Stokes: 6-1, 194, 4.31 40, RAS 9.35. Georgia. Blazing speed, developmental. Struggled post-injury.
2023 7.232 — Carrington Valentine: 6-0, 193, 4.44 40, RAS 9.29. Kentucky. 3-year starter. Press-man corner. Emerged as starter.
2018 2.45 — Josh Jackson: 6-0, 196, 4.56 40. Iowa. Production-based pick (8 INTs). Didn't pan out — slowest CB Gutekunst has drafted.
2024 7.255 — Kalen King: Penn State. High-upside late flier.

QB.archetype_summary: The Packers have a documented organizational philosophy of drafting QBs even when they have a franchise starter — Rodgers behind Favre, Love behind Rodgers. The archetype is an athletic, young, high-ceiling QB with dual-threat ability. Jordan Love (6-4, 224, RAS 8.46) was a project pick who sat for 3 years. They value arm talent, mobility, and youth over immediate readiness. Sullivan (now MIA GM) said: 'If you can help it, don't wait till you don't have a quarterback to find one.' Gutekunst is likely to draft a developmental QB in 2026 given Malik Willis departing in free agency.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-2 to 6-4. Love is 6-4. They hosted Jalon Daniels (6-0) at the 2026 combine, suggesting some flexibility on height.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Mobility and athleticism matter — Love's RAS 8.46 reflects good overall athleticism. They are reportedly interested in Taylen Green (elite athleticism score projected). Arm strength is a prerequisite.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a timed-speed position but must have enough mobility to extend plays and be a run threat. Love had rushing ability in college.

playing_style.primary_style: Athletic dual-threat with enough arm talent to push the ball downfield. Not a pure pocket passer, not a pure runner — a balanced quarterback with upside.
most_valued_traits: Arm strength and ability to make all the throws
Mobility and athleticism to extend plays
Youth and developmental ceiling
Ability to read defenses (developing, not necessarily ready-made)
least_important_traits: Immediate readiness — Love sat 3 years. The Packers are willing to develop QBs.
deal_breakers: Lack of arm talent
Below-average athleticism for the position
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Franchise QB upside — traded up in the first round for Love when no one expected it, despite having Rodgers in MVP form

college_production.production_floor: Love had a down senior season (20 TDs, 17 INTs) and the Packers still drafted him 26th overall. College production is secondary to physical tools and upside at QB.
college_production.production_trajectory: Not a major factor — Love's production actually declined in his final year and they still drafted him in round 1
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Love's supporting cast at Utah State was poor. Gutekunst clearly credited context.

examples: 2020 1.26 — Jordan Love: 6-4, 224, RAS 8.46. Utah State. Traded up from 30. Controversial at the time — Rodgers was still elite. Now the franchise QB.
2023 6.? — Sean Clifford: Penn State. Late-round developmental backup. Cut after 1 year.
2024 7.245 — Michael Pratt: Tulane. 4-year starter, AAC Offensive Player of the Year. Developmental backup.
2025 interest — Hosted Jalon Daniels (Kansas) at combine for 2026 draft evaluation.

QB.confidence_note: Only 1 premium QB drafted (Love). Strong organizational philosophy provides additional signal. The late-round QB picks (Clifford, Pratt) confirm the habit of always having a developmental arm.

OT.archetype_summary: Versatile, athletic offensive tackle who can play multiple positions. Jordan Morgan (6-5, 311, RAS 9.25) was drafted 25th overall in 2024 from Arizona as a left tackle who could also play guard. Rasheed Walker (6-6, 313) was a Day 3 pick who developed into a starter. Anthony Belton (6-6, 336) in 2025 round 2 was a massive prospect drafted for his size and run-blocking power despite being viewed as a reach. The Packers want big, athletic OTs with positional versatility.

physical_profile.weight_range: 311–336. Belton at 336 is notably heavy. Gutekunst said Belton has 'great size and can move people in the run game.'
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34 inches preferred for outside tackle play
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: RAS matters — Morgan 9.25 RAS. Athletic movement skills in space for zone-blocking scheme. Walker developed despite modest combine numbers, showing film can override testing.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: N/A for tackles, but must be athletic enough for zone-blocking reach blocks and pull blocks in LaFleur's scheme

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-scheme versatile tackle who can pass protect and reach-block in the run game. LaFleur's offense demands athletic OL who can get to the second level.
most_valued_traits: Positional versatility — ability to play tackle and guard (Morgan played both)
Athletic movement in space
Size and strength combination
Youth and developmental ceiling
Pass protection anchor
least_important_traits: Pure power/mauling run blocking without athletic component — scheme requires movement skills
deal_breakers: Lack of foot speed for zone scheme
Inability to play multiple positions
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Multi-position versatility combined with elite size. Belton was considered a reach at 54 but had 14 top-30 visits with teams.

college_production.production_floor: Wants multi-year starters. Morgan started 45+ games. Walker started 12 games at LT his senior year. Belton had 32 starts.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending trajectory — Morgan earned first-team All-Pac-12 as a senior
college_production.context_sensitivity: Weighs conference strength — all recent OT picks from Power 5 programs

examples: 2024 1.25 — Jordan Morgan: 6-5, 311, RAS 9.25. Arizona. Left tackle, can play guard. Multi-year starter.
2025 2.54 — Anthony Belton: 6-6, 336. NC State. Massive tackle with run-blocking power. 32 starts at LT.
2022 5.140 — Rasheed Walker: 6-6, 313. Penn State. Started every game in 2024 at RT. Day 3 development success.
2022 4.132 — Zach Tom: 6-5, 304. Wake Forest. Ultra-versatile — played tackle and guard. Started every game 2024.
2025 7.250 — John Williams: 6-4, 322. Cincinnati. Developmental OT/guard prospect.

IOL.archetype_summary: Versatile interior lineman who can play multiple positions along the line. Elgton Jenkins (2019, Rd 2) is the prototype — started at LG, C, and RT in a single season and made the Pro Bowl. Sean Rhyan (2022, Rd 3) played guard and center. Jacob Monk (2024, Rd 5) played RG, RT, and C in college. The Packers prize positional versatility above all else at IOL. Gutekunst has explicitly stated: 'We've always strived here to have guys that can play versatile roles.'

physical_profile.height_range: 6-3 to 6-5. Jenkins 6-5, Rhyan 6-5, Jon Runyan Jr 6-4, Josh Myers 6-5.
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict cutoff — values overall frame and power
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Movement skills are critical for zone scheme — must be able to pull and reach-block. Jenkins' ability to play 4 different positions demonstrates the athletic standard.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Must be mobile enough for outside zone scheme. Heavy-footed, anchor-only guards not valued.

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-scheme guard/center with positional flexibility. Must be able to get to the second level and block in space.
most_valued_traits: Multi-position versatility — this is THE defining trait GB seeks
Athletic movement in LaFleur's zone scheme
Physical strength and nastiness in run game
Intelligence to play center if needed
Durability
least_important_traits: Pure anchor-and-hold strength without mobility — the scheme demands movement
deal_breakers: One-position-only players with limited scheme fit
Lack of movement skills for zone blocking

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starters preferred. Jenkins started 3 years at Mississippi State. Monk started at 3 positions at Duke.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending. Rhyan was a multi-year starter at UCLA.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Considers conference strength and scheme fit

examples: 2019 2.44 — Elgton Jenkins: 6-5, 310. Mississippi St. 2x Pro Bowl. Played LG, C, RT in same season. Prototype.
2022 3.92 — Sean Rhyan: 6-5, 321. UCLA. Guard who can play center. Started every game in 2024.
2020 6.209 — Jon Runyan Jr: 6-4, 307. Michigan. Became starting LG. Versatile.
2021 2.62 — Josh Myers: 6-5, 310. Ohio State. Center. Let go after rookie deal.
2024 5.163 — Jacob Monk: Duke. Played RG, RT, and C. 5-year starter. Versatility pick.
2020 4.136 — Simon Stepaniak: 6-4, 314. Indiana. OL pick that didn't pan out (retired).
2020 6.192 — Jake Hanson: 6-5, 296. Oregon. Center depth pick.

DT.archetype_summary: Explosive, athletic interior disruptor. Devonte Wyatt (2022, Rd 1) is the recent flagship pick — a 6-3, 304-lb Georgia DT with a 4.77 40 and RAS 9.59. Gutekunst values pass-rush upside from the interior over pure run-stuffing. TJ Slaton (2021, Rd 5) at 6-4, 330 represented the bigger run-stuffing archetype on Day 3. The Packers have historically leaned toward explosion and pass rush ability at DT, though the Gannon scheme may shift priorities toward a true nose tackle.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-2 to 6-4. Wyatt 6-3, Slaton 6-4. No DT drafted shorter than 6-2 3/8.
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear cutoff — but length is valued
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Explosion tests matter most. Wyatt: 4.77 40, RAS 9.59. The Packers drafted Wyatt for his athleticism from Georgia despite questions about production. 3-tech DTs need quickness; nose tackles need mass.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-5.00 40 for 3-technique. Less important for nose.

playing_style.primary_style: Penetrating 3-technique who disrupts the backfield. Under new DC Gannon, may need more two-gap nose capability.
most_valued_traits: First-step quickness and burst off the ball
Pass rush upside from the interior
Athleticism — explosive movement for size
Versatility to play multiple DL alignments
Motor and effort
least_important_traits: Space-eating run stopping without pass rush ability — Gutekunst has historically undervalued pure run stuffers in early rounds
deal_breakers: Lack of athletic upside — one-dimensional run stuffers without pass rush get pushed to Day 3
Stiff, non-explosive movement profile
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athletic testing at massive size (Wyatt's 4.77 at 304 lbs)

college_production.production_floor: Modest — Wyatt had 2.5 sacks his final year at Georgia but was still taken in round 1. Production is secondary to physical traits.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values upward trajectory but not required
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Wyatt played alongside other first-round DL at Georgia and his stats were suppressed by talent around him

examples: 2022 1.28 — Devonte Wyatt: 6-3, 304, 4.77 40, RAS 9.59. Georgia. Explosive 3-tech. Round 1 pick for athleticism.
2021 5.173 — TJ Slaton: 6-4, 330. Florida. Run-stuffing nose tackle on Day 3.
2023 3.? — Colby Wooden: Auburn. Versatile DL. 3-tech type.
2023 5.? — Karl Brooks: Bowling Green. Developmental.
2025 6.198 — Warren Brinson: Georgia. Run-stopping DT. Day 3 depth.

WR.archetype_summary: Two distinct archetypes: (1) Speed/deep threat with elite athleticism (Watson 6-4/208/4.36, Golden 5-11/195/4.29, MVS 6-4/206/4.37) and (2) Productive route runner with strong hands (Doubs 6-2/204/4.47, Reed 5-11/187/4.45). Gutekunst frequently doubles up at WR — 3 in 2018, 2 in 2022, 2 in 2023, 2 in 2025. He values both production AND speed, often drafting one of each type. He said of Golden: 'Obviously he has elite speed... arguably some of the best hands in the draft.' The height/size emphasis is notable — Watson (6-4), Savion Williams (6-4), Golden (5-11 but elite speed), MVS (6-4). They like big targets.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-11 to 6-4. Wide range. The Packers draft both smaller slot types (Reed 5-11) and big outside receivers (Watson 6-4, Williams 6-4). Size is valued for outside receivers.
physical_profile.weight_range: 187–222. Reed 187 at the low end, Savion Williams 222 at the high end. For outside WRs, 200+ preferred.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash is king. Watson 4.36, MVS 4.37, Golden 4.29. Speed is the most coveted trait. Scouts specifically noted Golden as 'the fastest WR in the draft.' Even Reed was reportedly timed under 4.40 by the Packers.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster for early-round WRs. Sub-4.40 gets premium attention. Doubs (4.47 pro day) is the 'slowest' successful WR pick.

playing_style.primary_style: Field-stretching speed threat who can also win at the catch point. Secondarily: productive slot receivers with YAC ability.
most_valued_traits: Elite speed / ability to stretch the field
Strong hands and contested catch ability
Versatility — can play multiple WR positions
Return ability (Golden returned kicks, Reed returned punts)
Route-running development potential
least_important_traits: Polished route tree entering the NFL — willing to develop raw speed players (Watson was a project from NDSU)
Elite college production is NOT required for speed types — Watson had modest stats from North Dakota State
deal_breakers: Lack of speed AND lack of size — a slow, small WR won't get drafted
Drop issues without compensating elite speed
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite 40 time. Watson was traded up for despite coming from FCS. Golden's 4.29 made him a first-round lock.

college_production.production_floor: Varies by archetype. For productive types: Doubs had back-to-back 1,000-yard seasons at Nevada. Reed had 1,000 yards at MSU. For speed types: Watson had only 36 catches as a junior at NDSU. Production matters more for route-runner types than speed types.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending production. Golden went from 584 yards as a freshman to 987 yards as a junior at Texas.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Watson's NDSU production was credited given the level of competition. Golden's numbers at Texas were weighted by strong conference play.

examples: 2025 1.23 — Matthew Golden: 5-11, 195, 4.29 40. Texas. 58 catches, 987 yards, 9 TDs. First WR in round 1 since 2002.
2022 2.34 — Christian Watson: 6-4, 208, 4.36 40. North Dakota St. Speed/athleticism pick from FCS. Led team in TD catches as rookie.
2022 4.132 — Romeo Doubs: 6-2, 204, 4.47 40 (pro day). Nevada. 2x 1,000-yard receiver. Production-based pick.
2023 2.50 — Jayden Reed: 5-11, 187, 4.45 40. Michigan St. 1,000-yard season. Slot/versatile receiver. Also a punt returner.
2025 3.87 — Savion Williams: 6-4, 222, 4.48 40. TCU. Big-bodied WR with rushing ability. 130 catches over 3 years.
2018 5.174 — Marquez Valdes-Scantling: 6-4, 206, 4.37 40. Speed deep threat. 4 years with Packers.
2023 5.? — Dontayvion Wicks: Virginia. Day 3 WR with athleticism.
2018 4.133 — J'Mon Moore: Missouri. 4.50 40. Did not pan out.
2021 3.85 — Amari Rodgers: 5-9, 212, 4.51 40. Clemson. Slot type. Bust — Gutekunst described as 'short, but not small.'

TE.archetype_summary: Athletic receiving tight end with development upside. Gutekunst doubled up at TE in 2023, drafting Luke Musgrave (Rd 2) and Tucker Kraft (Rd 3). Kraft has emerged as a legitimate weapon. The Packers want athletic TEs who can create mismatches in the passing game, not primarily blockers. Earlier TE picks (Sternberger Rd 3 2019, Deguara Rd 3 2020) were busts, creating a documented 'third-round TE curse' that Kraft broke.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Receiving ability and athleticism in space. Vertical jump and broad jump as explosion indicators for TEs. Speed enough to create mismatches vs. LBs.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.75 preferred for receiving TEs. Kraft and Musgrave both had adequate tested speed.

playing_style.primary_style: Receiving-first tight end who can flex out and create mismatches. Blocking is secondary.
most_valued_traits: Receiving ability — hands, route running, YAC
Athletic ability to create mismatches vs. linebackers
Red zone target capability
Versatility to align inline or in the slot
least_important_traits: Elite in-line blocking — Gutekunst has consistently drafted TEs for their receiving, not blocking
deal_breakers: Pure blocking TE without receiving upside — never drafted this type in early rounds
Slow, unathletic movement profile
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Receiving production combined with athleticism — Kraft had 58 receptions as a senior at South Dakota State

college_production.production_floor: Varies — Kraft was a prolific FCS producer. Musgrave had modest stats at Oregon State. Sternberger had 48 catches at Texas A&M.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Adjusts for level of competition — Kraft from FCS, Musgrave from Pac-12

examples: 2023 3.78 — Tucker Kraft: 6-5, 253. South Dakota St. 81 receptions in first 2 NFL seasons, emerging weapon. Best TE pick.
2023 2.42 — Luke Musgrave: 6-6, 250. Oregon State. Athletic receiving TE. Injuries limited impact.
2020 3.94 — Josiah Deguara: 6-2, 240. Cincinnati. H-back/fullback hybrid. Niche role player.
2019 3.75 — Jace Sternberger: 6-5, 251. Texas A&M. Receiving TE. Bust — off-field issues.

S.archetype_summary: Versatile, multi-role safety who can play multiple spots in the secondary. The 2024 draft was a watershed — Gutekunst drafted THREE safeties (Bullard Rd 2, Williams Rd 4, Oladapo Rd 5). Bullard (5-10, 199, 4.47 40) is a nickel/slot hybrid. Williams (6-0, 203) is a box/run-support safety. Oladapo (6-2, 213) is a traditional strong safety. The earlier Darnell Savage (2019 Rd 1) was a speed/range free safety (5-11, 198, 4.36 40, RAS 8.37). Gutekunst values versatility and speed at safety, with an emerging preference for multiple safety packages (3-safety looks) that the Gannon scheme will emphasize.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-10 to 6-2. Savage 5-11, Bullard 5-10, Williams 6-0, Oladapo 6-2. Wide range reflecting different sub-roles.
physical_profile.weight_range: 198–213. Savage 198, Oladapo 213. Heavier safeties for box role, lighter for coverage role.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed (Savage 4.36, Bullard 4.47). Range and closing speed matter for free safeties. Physical testing matters for box safeties (Oladapo's 91.3 PFF run defense grade).
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster for coverage safeties. Box safeties can be slightly slower if physical.

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile safety who can play multiple roles — free safety, strong safety, slot/nickel, or box. Gutekunst prizes positional flexibility.
most_valued_traits: Versatility to play multiple spots in the secondary
Speed and range for coverage
Run defense ability — Williams was specifically valued for run support
Ball skills and instincts
Tackling physicality
least_important_traits: Single-role limitation — a pure free safety who can't play in the box, or vice versa, is less valued
deal_breakers: Lack of versatility — one-dimensional safeties deprioritized
Poor speed for coverage roles
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Combination of coverage and run-support versatility. Bullard's versatility (played nickel and safety at Georgia) made him a round 2 pick.

college_production.production_floor: Moderate. Savage had 4 INTs over final 2 college seasons. Bullard had 4 INTs in 3 seasons. Williams had 4 career INTs.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending — Savage was the first DB off the board in 2019 after a breakout junior year
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — credits Georgia's Bullard playing in Kirby Smart's system, Williams in Oregon's competitive secondary

examples: 2019 1.21 — Darnell Savage: 5-11, 198, 4.36 40, RAS 8.37. Maryland. Traded up two 4th-round picks to get him. Free safety. Left after 5 years.
2024 2.58 — Javon Bullard: 5-10, 199, 4.47 40. Georgia. Defensive MVP of 2022 national championship. Nickel/safety hybrid.
2024 4.111 — Evan Williams: 6-0, 203. Oregon (via Fresno St). 5-year player. Box safety, run support specialist. Earned All-Rookie honors.
2024 5.169 — Kitan Oladapo: 6-2, 213. Oregon St. Strong safety. 91.3 PFF run defense grade. Physical tackler.
2018 3.? — Oren Burks: Vanderbilt. Safety/LB hybrid. Did not pan out as Packer.

LB.archetype_summary: Fast, sideline-to-sideline linebacker with coverage ability and blitz capacity. The two primary investments are Quay Walker (2022 Rd 1, from Georgia, RAS 9.63) and Edgerrin Cooper (2024 Rd 2, from Texas A&M). Both are speed-first LBs — Walker was drafted for his elite athleticism, Cooper for his coverage ability and playmaking. The Packers want LBs who can cover tight ends and running backs, not just stuff the run. Cooper was an All-Rookie selection. Under Gannon's 3-4 scheme with three-safety looks, only 1-2 LBs will be on the field at a time, making coverage ability paramount.

physical_profile.weight_range: 218–241. Cooper 225, Walker 241. Cooper is lean/fast, Walker is bigger.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed is paramount. Walker RAS 9.63. Cooper ran in the 4.5s. Gutekunst explicitly said he wanted the defense to 'get faster' before the Cooper pick. 3-cone drill for change of direction matters for coverage LBs.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.60 preferred. Speed and range are the defining traits.

playing_style.primary_style: Fast, coverage-capable off-ball linebacker who can chase sideline-to-sideline and match up with backs/TEs. Under Gannon, must also be instinctive and disciplined positionally.
most_valued_traits: Sideline-to-sideline range and play speed
Coverage ability against TEs and RBs
Blitz effectiveness — Cooper had 8 sacks and 17 TFLs as a senior
Instincts and football IQ — Gannon demands LBs who 'know where to be'
Tackling physicality
least_important_traits: Pure run-stuffing size — the Packers have moved away from thumper LBs. Walker's run defense has been inconsistent despite his size.
deal_breakers: Inability to cover in space — Walker's coverage struggles (120.8 passer rating when targeted in 2025) have made him expendable
Slow, non-athletic movement profile
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite speed + coverage ability. Cooper's combination made him the target despite being available later than expected.

college_production.production_floor: Cooper: 75+ tackles, 13+ TFL, 3+ sacks, INT, FF, FR in his rookie year. College production matters here — Cooper was the top-rated LB in the 2024 class.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending — Cooper had 8 sacks and 17 TFL as a senior

examples: 2022 1.22 — Quay Walker: 6-4, 241, RAS 9.63. Georgia. Elite athlete, raw. Started immediately but has been inconsistent. May not be re-signed.
2024 2.45 — Edgerrin Cooper: 6-1, 225. Texas A&M. Top LB in class. All-Rookie. 85.7 PFF grade. The new anchor at LB.
2024 3.91 — Ty'Ron Hopper: Missouri. Athletic LB with special teams value.
2018 3.88 — Oren Burks: 6-3, 233. Vanderbilt. Versatile S/LB tweener. Mainly special teams.
2019 5.150 — Ty Summers: TCU. Speed LB. Special teams contributor.

RB.archetype_summary: Two archetypes drafted: (1) Big power back (AJ Dillon 2020 Rd 2, 6-0, 247 — a true bruiser from Boston College) and (2) Explosive change-of-pace back with receiving ability (MarShawn Lloyd 2024 Rd 3, 5-9, 220, 4.46 40). Gutekunst is willing to invest Day 2 capital at RB but supplements heavily in free agency (Josh Jacobs signed 2024, Aaron Jones developed from Day 3 pick pre-Gutekunst). He values versatility and pass-catching ability from RBs in LaFleur's scheme.

physical_profile.weight_range: 220–247. Dillon was a uniquely massive back. Lloyd more typical at 220.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed matters — Lloyd's 4.46 was important. But Dillon was not a speed back (4.53). The key is that the RB fits a specific offensive role.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Depends on role. Speed backs need sub-4.50. Power backs can be slower.

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile back who can contribute in multiple phases. LaFleur's offense uses RBs as receivers and needs pass protection.
most_valued_traits: Versatility — rushing and receiving dual-threat
Fit within LaFleur's zone-running scheme
Pass protection ability
Durability and contact balance
Big-play ability
least_important_traits: Position-specific role limitation — Gutekunst described Lloyd as 'short, but not little,' valuing his well-rounded skill set
deal_breakers: One-dimensional skill set without receiving or pass protection ability
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite yards-per-carry efficiency combined with receiving chops. Lloyd led FBS in YPC (7.1) at USC.

college_production.production_floor: No strict floor — Lloyd had modest total carries at USC (116). Dillon was a 3-year bellcow at BC with 1,685 yards as a sophomore.
college_production.production_trajectory: Varies — Dillon was a proven workhorse. Lloyd was more of an efficiency/upside bet.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — credits YPC and usage context

examples: 2020 2.62 — AJ Dillon: 6-0, 247, 4.53 40. Boston College. Pure power back. Controversial pick at the time. 4-year Packer.
2024 3.88 — MarShawn Lloyd: 5-9, 220, 4.46 40. USC. Explosive, versatile. 7.1 YPC as senior. Disappointing rookie year.
2020 7.236 — Dexter Williams: 5-11, 212. Notre Dame. Late flier, did not contribute.

reach_tolerance.notes: Gutekunst is comfortable reaching for athletic traits. Rashan Gary at 12 was a significant reach over consensus given his modest production. AJ Dillon at 62 was widely viewed as a reach for a RB. Quay Walker at 22 was debatable. Belton at 54 in 2025 was considered a reach (projected 3rd-4th round). Van Ness at 13 was seen as slight overdraft. The pattern is clear: Gutekunst will reach for physical traits and upside, especially in rounds 1-2.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Matt LaFleur's outside zone/play-action offense derived from the Shanahan/McVay coaching tree. Values athletic, versatile offensive linemen who can reach-block and get to the second level. QB mobility is valued. Deep passing game emphasis with speed receivers. TE usage growing (Kraft emerging). RBs need to catch passes and pass protect.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Transitioning to Jonathan Gannon's system for 2026 — 3-4 base with quarters coverage emphasis, three-safety looks (~30% of snaps), disguised coverages, and a preference for generating pressure with the front four rather than heavy blitzing. Previously ran Jeff Hafley's 4-3 base Cover 3/Cover 2 for 2024-2025. The scheme change is significant and will impact draft priorities — need versatile safeties, coverage LBs, and edge rushers who can play in a 3-4.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: Defensive coordinator changed from Jeff Hafley (departed to Miami as HC) to Jonathan Gannon (hired Feb 2, 2026). Transitioning from 4-3 base to 3-4 base. Gannon's scheme emphasizes multiple safety packages and coverage disguises. This will likely influence 2026 draft priorities: CB is the #1 need, DT/nose tackle needed for 3-4, coverage-capable LBs valued. Micah Parsons fits the 3-4 OLB role well.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate-to-aggressive risk taker. Gutekunst's signature moves are high-risk, high-reward: drafting Love at 26 while Rodgers was in MVP form, trading for Parsons at the cost of two 1sts, drafting raw athletic prospects (Gary, Watson, Wyatt) over polished college performers. He consistently bets on upside and traits over floor and production. His 'project pick' tendency has been widely documented — he 'frequently drafts project players in early and late rounds.' However, he has learned from misses and the 2024 draft (Cooper, Bullard, Williams) featured more polished players.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate recency bias. After losing to the 49ers in the 2021 divisional round partly due to special teams, he invested in special teams contributors. After the secondary was exposed in 2023-2024, he drafted 3 safeties. After WR production stalled, he finally took a WR in round 1 (2025). He responds to roster weaknesses but not in a panicky way — the doubling-up strategy is more systematic than reactive.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Moves on from busts relatively quickly. Let Savage, Stokes, Myers, Burks, and others depart after their rookie deals without overpaying. Did not double down on positions where he'd already invested and missed. However, extended Rashan Gary to a $96M deal after his ACL, which could be viewed as sunk-cost behavior. Mixed signals.
psychological_profile.combine_description: One of the most measurables-driven GMs in the NFL. 7 of 9 first-round picks had RAS of 9.0+. Gutekunst has some sort of 'height-weight-speed system that it typically sticks to in the draft' per reporting. Has hard cutoffs at certain positions (CB height, CB speed). However, he insists he doesn't use RAS specifically and says he won't let a fast 40 or bad vertical drastically change his board. The truth is somewhere in between — measurables are eliminators and confirmers, not the sole driver.
psychological_profile.character_description: Moderate character emphasis. Has taken chances on some character-question players (Amari Rodgers) and cut them for off-field reasons. The Wolf/Thompson tree historically had high character standards. Gutekunst maintains a baseline but is not as rigid as some GMs — he's willing to take Day 3 fliers on players with minor concerns.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Organizational philosophy is to always have a developmental QB in the pipeline. Drafted Rodgers behind Favre, Love behind Rodgers, Clifford and Pratt as late-round backups. Sullivan explicitly stated: 'don't wait till you don't have a quarterback to find one.' With Willis likely departing as a free agent in 2026, expect Gutekunst to draft a developmental QB again this cycle, likely on Day 2 or Day 3.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Leans heavily toward projection/traits over production for most positions. Gary (3.5 sacks his last year), Wyatt (2.5 sacks), Love (down senior year), Watson (FCS production) — all were picks where traits trumped stats. Exception is WR, where production is weighted higher: Doubs had 2x 1,000-yard seasons, Golden had 987 yards, Reed had 1,000+ yards. At CB, production is nearly irrelevant — Stokes had 1 INT. Gutekunst's draft room comments frequently emphasize physical attributes over statistical performance.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: Production matters more here than any other position. Packers evaluators specifically mentioned WR stats in post-draft pressers. Doubs and Reed were productive. Watson was the exception as a speed/traits pick from FCS.
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: No production minimum — Gary's 3.5 sacks as a senior didn't deter a top-12 pick. Traits dominate.
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear statistical floor — film over interception totals. Stokes had 1 INT in final college season.
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: Some production expected — Cooper was the top-rated LB with 8 sacks and 17 TFL. But Walker's modest stats at Georgia didn't hurt.
production_thresholds_by_position.QB: Production secondary to tools — Love's declining stats in his final year didn't deter a first-round selection.

college_production_preferences.notes: Gutekunst's evaluation philosophy is clearly traits-first, production-second. The post-draft quotes from Gutekunst and his staff emphasize physical attributes ('twitchy', 'explosive', 'dynamic athlete', 'size and speed', 'big and fast') more than any statistical achievements. The one exception is WR, where they consistently reference production numbers.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly hard cutoffs for CB (height, weight, speed). Softer preferences at other positions. The round 1 RAS threshold of 9.0+ has been violated only once (Jordan Love at 8.46, who was a QB). For non-QB positions in round 1, 9.0+ RAS appears to be a near-hard cutoff.

size_preferences_by_position.CB: 5-10 1/4 minimum height, 184+ lbs minimum weight. Historically the most rigid size requirements.
size_preferences_by_position.WR: No strict minimum but outside receivers tend to be 6-2+. Slot receivers can be smaller (Reed 5-11).
size_preferences_by_position.LB: No strict minimum — Cooper 6-1/225, Walker 6-4/241. Wide range accepted.
size_preferences_by_position.S: No strict minimum — Bullard 5-10/199, Oladapo 6-2/213. Varies by role.

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: RAS (Relative Athletic Score) is the single best proxy for what Gutekunst values, even though he says he doesn't use it. 40-yard dash is the most important individual test across positions. Vertical jump and broad jump serve as explosion indicators. 3-cone matters for EDGE and LB evaluations. The Packers appear to have an internal height-weight-speed composite system that functions similarly to RAS.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Measurables serve as eliminators — below-threshold athletes are eliminated regardless of film. But among players who clear the athletic threshold, film determines the final ranking. Example: Josh Jackson (CB, 2018) was drafted for production (8 INTs at Iowa) despite being the slowest CB Gutekunst has drafted — his film was exceptional. He busted, which may have reinforced the athletic threshold approach. Gutekunst's 'film first but athletes only' philosophy is well-documented.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Premium positions + elite athletes. 7 of 10 first-round picks were defensive players. RAS 9.0+ is nearly mandatory. Average age at pick: 21.3 years. Willing to trade up aggressively (4 of 6 first-round trades were moves up). Gutekunst targets the intersection of positional value + elite measurables + youth. In 2026, he has no first-round pick due to the Parsons trade.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: This is where the BPA-to-need shift happens most dramatically. Drafted skill position players on offense 6 straight times in round 2 (2019-2023), then pivoted to defense when secondary became the biggest need (2024). Doubles up at positions of need — 2 WRs in 2022, 2 TEs in 2023, 2 LBs in 2024. In 2026, with no 1st-round pick, rounds 2-3 become critical. Expect the first pick (~52nd) to address the biggest need (likely CB or DT).
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, OL depth, and special teams contributors. Has found legitimate starters on Day 3: Zach Tom (Rd 4, 2022 — now starting OT), Romeo Doubs (Rd 4, 2022), Carrington Valentine (Rd 7, 2023 — starting CB), Jon Runyan Jr (Rd 6, 2020 — starting OG). Also takes late-round QBs (Clifford Rd 6, Pratt Rd 7). Day 3 picks still tend toward athletes — Gutekunst quoted as saying 'there are certain athletic traits you have to have' even for later picks.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: Aware of comp pick implications but doesn't let them drive decisions. Gutekunst explicitly said: 'It's about winning now. If there's players who we can sign that give us the opportunity to win now more so than holding out for a compensatory pick in 2027, that's the decision we're going to make.'

EDGE.note: Gary has been the flagship hit. Van Ness still developing. Parsons acquired via trade.

CB.note: Alexander is elite. Stokes has been disappointing. Valentine emerging as Day 3 steal. Josh Jackson bust.

OL.note: Jenkins, Tom, Rhyan, Runyan, Walker all became starters. Myers and others didn't last. Strong overall.

WR.note: Watson, Doubs, Reed are the hits. MVS was useful. Multiple busts (J'Mon Moore, Amari Rodgers).

S.note: Savage was decent. Bullard and Williams both All-Rookie. Strong recent hit rate.

LB.note: Cooper is an emerging star. Walker is inconsistent. Burks and Summers were ST-only.

DT.note: Wyatt is a rotational starter at best. Slaton, Wooden, Brooks all depth. Weakest position historically.

TE.note: Kraft is the hit. Musgrave, Sternberger, Deguara all disappointments or busts. Third-round TE curse.

RB.note: Dillon was a solid contributor for 4 years. Lloyd had a bad rookie year.

QB.note: Love is the franchise QB — the biggest hit. Clifford and Pratt were late-round backups who didn't stick.

blind_spots: DT evaluation has been inconsistent — Wyatt was a first-round pick but hasn't been a difference-maker. Interior DL has been addressed more through free agency (Jarran Reed, Kenny Clark retention).
TE evaluation in rounds 2-3 was poor until Kraft — Sternberger, Deguara, Musgrave all underwhelmed.
Third-round picks overall have been a documented weakness — referred to as the 'third-round curse' by Packers media.
strengths: OL evaluation is elite — Jenkins, Tom, Rhyan, Runyan, Walker all developed into starters from various rounds.
Safety evaluation has improved dramatically — the 2024 triple-safety draft (Bullard, Williams, Oladapo) has been highly successful.
Late-round CB finds — Valentine (Rd 7) and Ballentine (UDFA) have both contributed, showing the evaluation staff can find value at CB on Day 3.

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: More active than predecessor Ted Thompson but still draft-focused. Has made significant FA signings (Za'Darius Smith/Amos 2019, Jacobs/McKinney 2024, Banks/Hobbs 2025) to address holes the draft hasn't filled. The 2024 McKinney signing (All-Pro) and Jacobs signing (Pro Bowl) were home runs. Uses FA to fill 1-2 major starting-caliber holes per year.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When a veteran starter is signed at a position, Gutekunst de-prioritizes that position in the early draft rounds but may still add depth later. Signed McKinney → still drafted 3 safeties (depth/future). Signed Jacobs → still drafted Lloyd as developmental backup. Does not stop drafting a position entirely after FA, but shifts timing to Day 2-3.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at premium positions (QB, EDGE, CB, OT), supplement through FA at non-premium positions (RB, S, IOL). The Parsons trade represents a new willingness to acquire elite talent via blockbuster trades. Still fundamentally a draft-and-develop organization but increasingly willing to use all acquisition methods. Has built the youngest roster in the NFL (weighted age 25.58 in 2023, 25.72 in 2024).

notable_patterns: 7 of 10 first-round picks have been defensive players
8 of 9 first-round picks (pre-Golden) had RAS of 8.0+, 7 had 9.0+
8 of 9 first-round picks were 22 or younger at draft time
Consistently doubles up at positions of need (2+ picks at same position group in a single draft)
Traded up in the first round in 4 of 6 drafts where he had a first-round pick
Never drafted a WR in round 1 until 2025 (23-year drought)
Has drafted at least one OL in every draft since becoming GM
Frequently drafts players from Georgia (Walker, Wyatt, Bullard, Brinson — 4+ Bulldogs drafted)
Day 3 OL picks have been exceptionally productive (Tom, Runyan, Walker, Rhyan)

biggest_reaches.context: Gary had only 3.5 sacks as a senior at Michigan. Drafted for being the #1 recruit and elite RAS 9.95. Many expected him to go later. Justified — now a Pro Bowler.
Controversial pick with Rodgers still on roster. Traded up to get him. Production had declined. Now the franchise QB.
Round 2 RB when the team had Aaron Jones. Widely criticized. Contributed as a power complement for 4 years.
Projected 3rd-4th round. LaFleur revealed Belton attended 14 top-30 visits. Massive OT. Played RG as rookie.

biggest_steals.context: Versatile OL from Wake Forest. Now starts every game at OT. Day 3 steal.
7th-round CB from Kentucky. Now a starting cornerback. RAS 9.29. Classic Packers late-round CB find.
Productive WR from Nevada. Immediate starter. Career bests in receiving as a 4th-year pro.
Top LB in the class fell to 45. All-Rookie honors. 85.7 PFF grade. Could be the best pick of the class.

confidence_notes: 8 drafts of data as Packers GM provides a robust sample. Extensive public statements, post-draft pressers, and media coverage corroborate behavioral patterns. Key uncertainty: the Gannon defensive scheme change may shift defensive draft priorities for 2026. Also, the departure of VP of Player Personnel Jon-Eric Sullivan to Miami introduces some uncertainty about internal evaluation dynamics. Entering the 2026 draft without a first-round pick for the first time in Gutekunst's tenure adds a unique wrinkle.
